:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 895 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 6% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Parole is available to certain inmates who are serving 6-month or longer sentences and have served more than one-fourth of their aggregate sentence. However, an inmate not serving a sentence for a crime of violence or for certain drug crimes may be released on parole at any time to undergo drug or alcohol treatment, mental health treatment, or to participate in a residential program in the best interest of an expected or newborn child. If serving an aggregate sentence where there is a period of no parole eligibility related to one of the sentences, a parolee can be released when they have completed the greater of one-fourth of the aggregate sentence or the period of no parole eligibility. For example, if a person is sentenced to 20 years with standard parole eligibility and another 10 year sentence with parole eligibility after 4 years, they will have to wait five years (20 years * 1/4 = 5 years) to be eligible for release.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 79% (608 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 24 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 3% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
